reason: OR(1,1) = 1, so the output is One.
NOR(1,1) = NOT(OR(1,1)) = NOT(1) = 0, so the output is Zero.
In an RS latch, R stands for Reset and S stands for Set.
R=1, S=0 asserts Reset, driving Q to 0; 'output is reset to zero' is the correct phrasing (option C's 'set to zero' is a decoy).
The stem is the standard textbook definition of a DBMS, so True.
A data dictionary is a computer-based catalog/directory holding metadata about the database.
SQL expands to Structured Query Language.
Metadata is by definition data about data.
ANSI/SPARC three-schema architecture comprises the internal, conceptual, and external levels.
Schemas are defined with the Data Definition Language (DDL); DML is Data Manipulation Language, not schema definition.
MS-Access is a desktop RDBMS used to create databases, so True (labelled B here).
MS-Excel is a spreadsheet application, so True.
A file is a collection of related records/data, so True (labelled B here).
The classic four organizations are sequential, index-sequential, random, and multi-key; option A plus option B covers exactly those, so '1 and 2'.
Standard data file types include master/transaction/history and table/control/report; options 1 and 3 together, so 'D'. Option B (large/medium/small) is nonsense.
A block is the smallest unit of data transferred to/from secondary storage in one access.
Physical write is the actual transfer of a block from the buffer out to the external file, as opposed to a logical write into the buffer; True.
Matches the usual course definition of the blocking/deblocking buffer routine, so True; strictly, delivering one record at a time from a block is DE-blocking, so the stem's terminology is loose.
Odd parity forces an ODD count of 1 bits; the stem says even, so the statement is False (labelled A here).
A secondary key is any non-primary-key field used as an access key; the other options are positional misreadings.
